analysis.problem: Реки по целия свят загубиха кислород, което води до загадъчно загиване на водни екосистеми
analysis.root_cause: Климатичните промени водят до увеличено разрушаване на водните екосистеми, което намалява присъствието на кислород в реките
leverage_points: Международни организации и научни институции, които следят и регулират климатичните промени
Граждански организации и активисти, които изискват прозрачност и отговорност от правителствата

proposed_actions.action: Изработване и прилагане на строго регулирана политика за намаляване на емисиите на парникови газове
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Съкращаване на емисиите на CO2 с 50% до 2035 г.

proposed_actions.action: Инвестиции в изследвания и технологии за подобряване на кислородната екосистема в реките
proposed_actions.measurable_goal: Изработване на минимално 3 нови технологии за подобряване на кислородната концентрация в реките до 2030 г.

raw_items.title: Scientists warn that the world’s rivers are running out of oxygen
raw_items.summary: Rivers around the world are quietly running out of oxygen — and climate change is emerging as the main culprit. A sweeping global analysis of more than 21,000 river systems found that nearly 80% have been steadily losing dissolved oxygen over the past four decades, threatening fish, biodiversity, and the overall health of freshwater ecosystems. Surprisingly, tropical rivers are being hit the hardest, even more than rivers in rapidly warming polar regions.

raw_items.title: Scientists discover hidden “brakes” that stop massive earthquakes
raw_items.summary: A mysterious underwater fault near Ecuador has been producing nearly identical magnitude 6 earthquakes every five to six years, baffling scientists for decades. Researchers now believe the fault contains hidden “brake zones” where seawater and unusual rock structures work together to stop quakes from becoming even larger. The discovery came from ultra-detailed seafloor recordings that captured how the fault behaves before and after major earthquakes.

raw_items.title: Deadly “red sky” solar storm from 800 years ago discovered in ancient trees
raw_items.summary: Researchers in Japan traced a hidden medieval solar storm using ancient tree rings and centuries-old sky observations. The team linked reports of eerie red auroras with spikes of carbon-14 trapped in buried wood, revealing a powerful solar radiation event around 1200 CE. The findings suggest the Sun was far more active at the time, with unusually short solar cycles.

raw_items.title: Giant squid discovery uncovers a hidden deep-sea world off Australia
raw_items.summary: Scientists exploring deep underwater canyons off the coast of Western Australia uncovered a hidden world packed with bizarre and elusive marine life — including signs of the legendary giant squid. By analyzing traces of DNA floating in seawater from depths exceeding 4 kilometers, researchers identified 226 species ranging from deep-diving whales to strange fish rarely or never seen in the region before. Some of the creatures may even be unknown to science.

raw_items.title: Geoengineering
raw_items.summary: Geoengineering (also known as climate engineering or climate intervention) is the deliberate large-scale interventions in the Earth’s climate system intended to counteract human-caused climate change. The term commonly encompasses two broad categories: large-scale carbon dioxide removal (CDR) and solar radiation modification (SRM). raw_items.title: About Geoengineering | US EPA
raw_items.summary: April 3, 2026 - Geoengineering refers to activities intended to cool the Earth or remove greenhouse gases from the air and has health and environmental impacts. This page summarizes geoengineering and potential impacts.

raw_items.title: Planetary boundaries
raw_items.summary: Planetary boundaries are a framework to describe limits to the impacts of human activities on the Earth system. Beyond these limits, the environment may not be able to continue to self-regulate. raw_items.summary: 3 weeks ago - The planetary boundaries framework highlights the rising risks from human pressure on nine critical global processes that regulate the stability and resilience of the Earth
